title: Dynamic Programming Basics
learningObjectives: Explain Dynamic Programming Basics with interview-grade clarity
Implement Dynamic Programming Basics solutions in clean Java
Choose the right pattern and justify complexity
Handle boundary cases without brittle logic
Use this topic to solve coding-round problems confidently

sections.title: Why Dynamic Programming Basics matters
sections.content:
Dynamic programming is about recognizing overlapping subproblems and turning repeated work into cached reuse.

This day is central to DSA interviews because it combines coding speed with reasoning quality. Interviewers usually evaluate three things:
1. Can you identify the right pattern quickly?
2. Can you explain why the approach is correct?
3. Can you reason about complexity and edge cases?

State definition, transition logic, and memoization/tabulation choice.

A high-quality answer should include:
- concise approach summary,
- small dry-run,
- complexity analysis,
- one alternative approach and trade-off.

By the end of Day 27, you should be able to solve representative problems and explain your decisions with confidence.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Dynamic Programming Basics
sections.content:
### Why this topic matters now
Dynamic programming is about recognizing overlapping subproblems and turning repeated work into cached reuse.

### Core interview mental model
For Dynamic Programming Basics, first identify the problem family, then map it to a known pattern. Pattern-first thinking reduces random trial-and-error.

### Implementation guidance
1. Write the simplest correct version first.
2. Validate using one normal and one edge case.
3. Improve complexity only after correctness is locked.
4. Refactor to readable helper methods.

### Complexity and trade-off lens
Do not state complexity by memory. Derive it from operations performed. Mention time + space and where each cost comes from.

### Common failure mode
Most failures in Dynamic Programming Basics are not syntax errors. They come from broken invariants, weak boundary checks, or unclear state tracking.

### Interview speaking template
- "I considered X because..."
- "The invariant is..."
- "Per iteration we do..., so complexity is..."
- "An alternative would be..., but I chose this due to..."

### Production relevance
Even outside interviews, these patterns improve throughput, latency, and correctness in backend data processing tasks.

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**DP = optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems.** Top-down memo vs bottom-up table — same complexity, different stack use.

**State design:** dimensions are often index + constraint (knapsack weight); reduce state space when possible.

**Space optimization:** rolling array when transition only needs previous row.

**Interview:** start brute force recursion, identify repeated calls, add memo — shows structured thinking.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Fibonacci DP: memo table avoids exponential recomputation.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Classic 0/1 knapsack: 2D DP table for value vs weight.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: LIS with patience sorting / binary search — O(n log n) length.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Defining DP state ambiguously, causing wrong transitions.
Recursing without memoization and hitting exponential blow-up.
Using tabulation order that references uninitialized states.
Ignoring base-case initialization in DP arrays.
Optimizing space before validating transition correctness.

sections.title: Exercise - Dynamic Programming Basics Problem Drill
sections.problem:
Solve one medium-level Java problem for **Dynamic Programming Basics**.

Requirements:
1. Provide brute-force and optimized approach.
2. Explain why optimized approach is better.
3. Include one edge-case test.
4. Print expected output for sample input.
5. State time/space complexity clearly.
hints: Dry-run on paper for one non-trivial case.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is dynamic programming?
conceptual.answer: Break problem into overlapping subproblems; cache results to avoid exponential recomputation.

conceptual.question: Top-down vs bottom-up?
conceptual.answer: Memoization recursion vs iterative table filling—same math, different stack/space trade-offs.

conceptual.question: What is optimal substructure?
conceptual.answer: Optimal solution built from optimal solutions to subproblems.

conceptual.question: Classic 0/1 knapsack recurrence?
conceptual.answer: dp[i][w] max value using first i items with weight limit w—take or skip item.

conceptual.question: Unbounded knapsack difference?
conceptual.answer: Can reuse items—infinite loop variant in inner loop order.

conceptual.question: Fibonacci memo vs tabulation?
conceptual.answer: Memo O(n) stack depth; tabulation O(1) space rolling.

conceptual.question: Coin change variants?
conceptual.answer: Count ways vs min coins—different recurrences; infinite vs finite coins.

conceptual.question: Palindrome DP?
conceptual.answer: Table[i][j] whether substring palindrome—expand or fill diagonally.

conceptual.question: DAG shortest path as DP?
conceptual.answer: Topological order relaxation—linear time on DAG.

conceptual.question: When DP fails?
conceptual.answer: If optimal substructure or overlapping subproblems don’t hold—greedy may apply instead.

conceptual.question: Space optimization in DP?
conceptual.answer: If recurrence only needs previous row, keep two arrays or rolling variables.

conceptual.question: Digit DP (mention)?
conceptual.answer: Count numbers in range with digit constraints—competitive programming pattern.

codeBased.question: Fibonacci bottom-up O(n) time O(1) space.
Coin change min coins—BFS or DP.
LIS O(n log n) patience sorting.
Unique paths grid with obstacles.
codeBased.answer: dp[i][j] sum from top and left.

seniorScenario.question: DP solution too slow in production—n large?
seniorScenario.answer: Greedy proof, approximation algorithms, or mathematical closed form.

seniorScenario.question: Memory blow-up 2D DP table?
seniorScenario.answer: Rolling array, sparse representation, or switch algorithm.

seniorScenario.question: Scheduling weighted interval DP?
seniorScenario.answer: Sort by end time; dp[i] max profit—classic.

seniorScenario.question: ML hyperparameter grid search vs DP?
seniorScenario.answer: Different—avoid confusing ML tuning with algorithmic DP.

seniorScenario.question: Stack overflow in deep memo recursion?

wrongAnswers: DP always runs faster than brute force for every problem.
Greedy algorithms never work when DP applies.
Memoization and tabulation always use the same asymptotic time.
0/1 knapsack can be solved greedily by value/weight ratio always.
Fibonacci DP must keep entire table of size n.
Dynamic programming requires the problem to be NP-hard.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Day 27 Focus | Quick Reminder | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Dynamic Programming Basics | Choose pattern before coding | Random brute-force wandering |
| Correctness | Invariant + edge checks | Dry-run once | Off-by-one / null-state miss |
| Complexity | Derive, do not guess | Mention time + space | Incomplete complexity answers |
| Communication | Explain decisions | Use trade-off statement | Generic textbook responses |
| Quality | Readable Java methods | Stable naming + small units | Clever but fragile logic |
